text: This is a video of a sixty-two year old male with distal pancreatic mass measuring six by five centimeters. The patient had EOS complicated with perforation and peripancreatic inflammation and fluid collection. Pathology from EUS showed the neuroendocrine tumor.

This is a video of a sixty-two year old male with distal pancreatic mass measuring six by five centimeters. The patient had EOS complicated with perforation and peripancreatic inflammation and fluid collection. Pathology from EUS showed the neuroendocrine tumor.

The video shows a large pancreatic mass with close association to the duodenojejunal flexure. The spleen appears enlarged relative to the liver, while the colon is not closely proximate to the inflamed area.

The video shows the pancreatic mass in close association with the duodenojejunal flexure. The colon is not closely proximate to the inflamed area, and the spleen appears enlarged relative to the liver.
Patient has no history of portal hypertension
Patient has no history of portal hypertension
The robotic surgical procedure begins with the opening of the gastro-colic omentum, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The robotic surgery begins with the opening of the gastro-colic omentum, allowing entry into the lesser sac.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac and uses a vessel sealer to further open the gastrocolic omentum, bringing down the colon to expose the pancreas and the lesser sac.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to further open the gastrocolic omentum, bringing down the colon to expose the pancreas and the lesser sac. The dissection progresses towards the distal stomach, moving in the direction of the pylorus.

The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum towards the distal stomach, approaching the pylorus. Dense adhesions are visible between the stomach and pancreas, likely resulting from the previous EUS perforation.

The surgeon encounters significant adhesions between the stomach and pancreas, attributed to a previous EUS perforation. The inflammatory cavity between these structures is entered, and fluid is drained from the cavity.
The surgeon enters the inflammatory cavity located between the pancreas and the stomach, draining fluid from the cavity. This cavity is a result of complications from the EUS procedure.
The surgeon encounters necrotic tissue and proceeds to mobilize the colon, exposing the inferior border of the pancreas.
The surgeon focuses on mobilizing the colon to expose the inferior border of the pancreas, revealing necrotic tissue in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the colon, bringing it down slowly to avoid injury, while navigating through inflamed tissue. The pancreas becomes visible as the Maryland bipolar forceps are used to dissect and remove adhesions.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the colon, bringing it down slowly to avoid injury, while navigating through inflamed tissue. The pancreas becomes visible as the Maryland bipolar forceps are used to dissect and remove adhesions.
The surgeon exposes the DJ flexure at the level of the ligament of Treitz, performing careful lasso adhesion using cold scissors to separate the tissue.
The surgeon visualizes the DJ flexure at the level of the ligament of Treitz and performs careful lasso adhesion using cold scissors.

The surgeon inspects the colon and visualizes the middle colic artery, providing a reference for the location of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and the portal vein. Adhesions are further lysed until the duodenal flexure is completely separated from the inferior border of the pancreas. The inferior mesenteric vein is typically located at this level, entering the splenic vein.

The surgeon inspects the colon and visualizes the middle colic artery, indicating the location of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and portal vein. Adhesions are further dissected until the duodenal flexure is completely separated from the inferior border of the pancreas. The dissection continues beneath the pancreas towards the retropancreatic plane, which typically requires minimal dissection in a normal pancreas.
The surgeon continues dissection beneath the pancreas, advancing towards the retropancreatic plane. In a normal pancreas, this plane is typically undisturbed, requiring minimal dissection to access.

The surgeon uses a tip-up fenestrated instrument to lift the pancreas, while a laparoscopic ultrasound probe is employed to locate the tumor and assess the need for further dissection to ensure a negative margin.

The surgeon lifts the pancreas using a tip-up fenestrated instrument while employing a laparoscopic ultrasound probe to locate the tumor and assess the need for further dissection. Attention is focused on freeing the stomach from the superior border of the pancreas to dissect the splenic artery. A vessel sealer is used to remove the inflammatory wall between the stomach and pancreas. The robotic system provides ten-time magnification for clear visualization, aiding in careful dissection. A robotic suction irrigator assists in lysing adhesions and suctioning, as the colon is gently pulled away from potential harm.

The surgeon directs attention to freeing the stomach from the superior border of the pancreas, using a vessel sealer to dissect the inflammatory wall between these structures. The robotic system provides ten-time magnification for clear visualization, aiding in careful dissection. A robotic suction irrigator is employed for precise adhesion lysis and suctioning. The colon is gently pulled away from the surgical field.
The surgeon uses heat electrocautery to enter the inflammatory cavity, suctioning fluid from the cavity. The colon is safely repositioned, and attention shifts to forming the retropancreatic tunnel.
The surgeon uses heat electrocautery to enter the inflammatory cavity, suctioning fluid from the cavity. The colon is safely repositioned, and the focus shifts to forming the retropancreatic tunnel.
The surgeon identifies the inferior pancreatic artery arising from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). Two surgical clips are applied to the artery, and it is then transected.
The surgeon identifies the inferior pancreatic artery arising from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). Two clips are applied to the artery, and it is then transected.
The surgeon attempts to expose the splenic artery located on the superior border of the pancreas, carefully navigating through a thick inflammatory peel covering the area.

The surgeon exposes the splenic artery located on the superior border of the pancreas, carefully dissecting through a thick inflammatory peel. The dissection continues to ensure the artery is correctly identified and not mistaken for the common hepatic artery, with attention given to tracing the artery's path towards the pancreas.

The surgeon carefully dissects the artery superior to the pancreas, ensuring it is not mistaken for the common hepatic artery. The dissection continues until the hepatic artery is visualized or the artery's path into the pancreas is confirmed.

The surgeon clamps the artery, observing changes in liver color to ensure blood flow is not compromised. The spleen is inspected for signs of ischemia. Dissection beneath the artery is performed to encircle it with a vessel loop, which is then placed around the artery.

The surgeon clamps the artery, observing changes in liver color to ensure blood flow is not compromised, and inspects the spleen for signs of ischemia. Dissection beneath the artery is performed, encircling it with a vessel loop. The surgeon attempts dissection towards the common hepatic artery, facing challenges due to severe inflammation.
The surgeon attempts dissection towards the common hepatic artery, encountering difficulty due to severe inflammation surrounding the area.
The surgeon identifies the splenic vein located behind the pancreas and creates a tunnel between the splenic vein and the pancreas, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon identifies the splenic vein located behind the pancreas and forms a tunnel between the splenic vein and the pancreas using careful dissection techniques.
The surgeon passes a vessel loop around the artery, confirming through dissection that the looped vessel is the splenic artery as it dives into the pancreas, rather than the common hepatic artery.

A vessel loop is passed around the artery, and further dissection is carried out towards the pancreas to confirm that the looped vessel is the splenic artery, as it dives into the pancreas, distinguishing it from the common hepatic artery.

The surgeon uses a robotic vascular stapler to transect the confirmed splenic artery, removing the vessel loop. The splenic vein is clipped and transected. The procedure involves a distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy, with no intention of spleen preservation due to tumor size.

The surgeon removes the vessel loop and clips the splenic vein before transecting it. The procedure involves a distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy, with no intention of preserving the spleen due to the tumor size.

A vessel loop is passed behind the pancreas to elevate it for stapling. A green load 45 stapler is used to clamp the pancreas, utilizing smart technology to achieve the appropriate thickness. The clamp is held for three minutes before firing, successfully transecting the pancreas. The vessel loop remains on the specimen side, avoiding additional resection.

A vessel loop is passed behind the pancreas to elevate it for stapling. A green load of 45 staplers is used, and the pancreas is clamped with a stapler featuring smart technology. The surgeon adjusts the clamp to achieve the appropriate thickness before holding it for three minutes and firing, successfully transecting the pancreas. The vessel loop remains on the specimen side, avoiding the need for additional resection.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas in a medial to lateral fashion using a vessel sealer and Maryland bipolar forceps. The short gastric vessels are taken down, and the entire stomach is mobilized. The splenic attachment to the diaphragm is also detached.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas in a medial to lateral fashion using a vessel sealer and Maryland bipolar forceps. The short gastric vessels are taken down, and the entire stomach is mobilized. The splenic attachment to the diaphragm is also dissected and released.

The surgeon inspects the stomach for injuries by submerging it in saline, observing that it remains intact with no visible damage. The colon is also inspected and confirmed to be intact. A drain is placed in the left upper quadrant, and the pancreatic staple is left without reinforcement.
